summary: This notebook implements an inference pipeline for the Ariel Data Challenge 2025, combining rigorous custom signal processing with pre-trained neural networks. It calibrates raw sensor data, estimates per-planet noise levels using variance ratios, and predicts transit depth via a custom optimization routine. Pre-trained PyTorch ResNetMLPs then map stellar parameters to spectral fluxes, demonstrating a hybrid approach to astronomical data analysis. Readers should learn how to handle noisy sensor data, estimate uncertainties, and leverage pre-trained models for rapid baseline generation.
competition_flow: Raw sensor data is calibrated and binned, transit depth is estimated via custom optimization, pre-trained PyTorch ResNetMLPs predict spectral fluxes using stellar parameters, and results are combined with estimated noise levels to generate a submission CSV.
data_reading: Reads signal and calibration files (dark, dead, flat, linear_corr) from parquet files per planet and sensor.
Reads ADC gain/offset and star metadata from CSVs.
data_processing: ADC gain/offset correction and hot pixel removal via sigma clipping.
Linear correction, signal binning, variance-based weighting, and percentile clipping.
Savitzky-Golay filtering and custom sigma estimation using out-of-transit/in-transit variance ratios.
features_engineering: Stellar radius (Rs) and inclination (i) combined with the estimated transit depth to form the input feature vector for the neural networks.
insights: Careful calibration and variance weighting are critical for handling raw astronomical sensor data.
Estimating per-planet noise levels using out-of-transit and in-transit variance ratios improves uncertainty quantification.
Pre-trained shallow residual networks can effectively map stellar parameters to spectral fluxes when combined with robust signal processing.
